ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific Schezwan/Indo-Chinese fried rice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried rice as a category; no Schezwan-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Schezwan fried rice or Indo-Chinese fried rice dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Schezwan Fried Rice is approximately 350 g (one plate), as typically served in Indian-Chinese restaurants in Singapore and India.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, with egg and vegetables — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (adapted composite for Schezwan Fried Rice)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Schezwan Fried Rice specifically. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Fried rice, Chinese restaurant style' (FDC ID 169957) and ICMR-NIN data for egg fried rice and vegetable fried rice, adjusted upward for Schezwan sauce contribution (higher sodium, moderate additional fat from chilli oil). The composite represents the egg/chicken variant as the nutritionally dominant form. Veg variant would have slightly lower protein and energy; chicken variant slightly higher protein.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Fried rice (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Schezwan Fried Rice. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based fried rice dishes (GI range 48–65, central estimate 55 for stir-fried rice with mixed ingredients). The Schezwan sauce and vegetable content do not materially alter the GI relative to the rice base. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 26.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 51.4 g per 350 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Schezwan sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g serving of Schezwan Fried Rice provides approximately 1,680 mg of sodium, which represents 73–84% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sauce preparation, or avoid this dish on high-sodium days.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load from white rice base with moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of ~51 per serving, Schezwan Fried Rice poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and prefer the vegetable variant to reduce overall energy density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and egg/chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the vegetable variant, request less oil in preparation, and limit consumption to occasional servings within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Schezwan Fried Rice is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish widely served in Indian restaurants, street-food stalls, and delivery platforms across India and Singapore. It is prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a wok over high heat with Schezwan sauce — a fiery, garlicky condiment made from dried red chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, and vinegar — along with soy sauce, spring onions, and mixed vegetables such as capsicum, carrot, and cabbage. The dish is available in three common variants: vegetarian (veg), with egg, and with chicken. The egg variant incorporates scrambled or tossed egg for additional protein; the chicken variant adds diced or shredded cooked chicken. The texture is characteristically dry and slightly smoky from wok-hei, with a bold, spicy, and umami-rich flavour profile. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal providing approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from egg or chicken variants. Sodium content is notably high due to Schezwan sauce and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low-moderate), but the large serving size results in a high glycemic load per plate. This dish is a staple of Indo-Chinese cuisine, which emerged from the Chinese immigrant community in Kolkata, India, and has since become one of the most popular fusion food categories across South and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Schezwan Fried Rice?
What is the difference between veg, egg, and chicken Schezwan Fried Rice?
Can diabetics eat Schezwan Fried Rice?
Is Schezwan Fried Rice high in sodium?
What is Schezwan sauce made of?
Is Schezwan Fried Rice the same as Szechuan Fried Rice?
Schezwan Fried Rice GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Schezwan Fried Rice as a named dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fried rice entries and ICMR-NIN egg/vegetable fried rice data, adjusted for Schezwan sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level evidence is strong and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the egg/chicken variant as the nutritionally dominant form; the veg variant would have approximately 10–15% lower energy and protein. 2. Sodium is estimated at 480 mg/100 g reflecting typical Schezwan sauce and soy sauce usage; restaurant-specific sodium may vary significantly. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN source data for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this estimation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this estimation. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entries; no direct measurement for Schezwan Fried Rice exists in the approved GI literature. 7. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Grains and staples' as rice is the dominant macronutrient contributor. 8. Dietary flag is_halal is set to false as halal certification status of restaurant preparation cannot be confirmed from available data; individual restaurants may hold halal certification. 9. glycemic_index set to 55 (low category per ≤55 threshold); gi_category is 'low' per Veritas GI threshold rules.
